summary: Fernando Pérez presents Project Jupyter's evolution from a simple IPython terminal into a modular, protocol-based interactive computing platform serving science, education, and industry. He traces the journey from his 2001 grad-student procrastination project through the notebook's creation, multi-language kernel support, and the new JupyterLab architecture. He demonstrates how Jupyter enables reproducible research through tools like Binder (one-click executable papers), citing LIGO's gravitational wave analysis as a landmark example. The talk highlights JupyterHub deployments at universities and supercomputing centers, and previews JupyterLab's plugin-based architecture that bridges interactive exploration with large-scale HPC workflows.
key_points: Jupyter's core innovation was turning the read-eval-print loop into a well-defined network protocol, decoupling computation from display and enabling multi-language support across ~75 kernels
Binder and reproducible notebooks have moved from aspiration to reality, with LIGO releasing gravitational wave detection analysis as executable Jupyter notebooks anyone can re-run
JupyterLab represents a ground-up rearchitecture into a modular plugin system, replacing the organically-grown Frankenstein notebook interface with composable building blocks
JupyterHub enables institutional deployments for education (UC Berkeley's data science curriculum) and HPC centers (NERSC, SDSC), bridging the gap between laptops and supercomputers
The project thrives on a broad coalition of industry, academia, government, and open-source contributors, with deliberate architectural decisions enabling others to build on the platform
money_quotes: We have data. We've all seen slides like this about kind of the flood of data in every discipline. The question is, with all that data and all that compute power, we still have one of these. And it's not getting any bigger.

What we've tried to build with the tools in IPython and Jupyter is to be the bridge between our limited puny brains that are trying to make sense of the world and these silicon toys that we keep building and keep getting bigger.

We basically turned the read-about-print loop into a well-defined network protocol, where something on one end could execute code, and something at the other end would receive the results of that execution.

To first approximation, I like to describe it as Google Docs with a brain, because you have a document, you can type code, you can type text, you can format the text, you can type mathematics, you can also type code.

The LIGO collaboration now releases all of their analysis in the form of Jupyter notebooks, and you can literally click on it and execute, re-execute the LIGO gravitational wave detection analysis yourself without ever installing or downloading absolutely anything.
We are certainly not experts, but we acknowledge the severity of what we're doing. And we invite experts in security to join us.

What we've done is we've weaponized JavaScript, right? This is weapons-grade JavaScript connected in the browser, connected directly with APIs that hook all the way back to remote resources with arbitrary code execution.
key_insight: By abstracting the interactive computing loop into a modular network protocol, Jupyter evolved from a personal productivity hack into infrastructure that makes reproducible, collaborative science a practical reality across languages, institutions, and scales.
